title: Book Review: "Power and Progress"
gist: Noah Smith's highly critical, previously-paywalled 2024 review of Acemoglu and Johnson's Power and Progress argues the book's history is cherry-picked, its definition of 'power' is tautological, and its central policy idea of 'steering' innovation toward labor-complementing technologies is empirically unworkable and now newly relevant since Acemoglu got the same idea inserted into the 'We Must Act Now' AI statement.

themes.theme: Why this old review matters now

insights.claim: Daron Acemoglu got the organizers of the 2026 'We Must Act Now: A Statement on AI's Transformation of the Economy' to alter its language before he would sign, most likely adding the idea that policymakers must 'steer' AI in a direction that complements humans.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Acemoglu's 'steering' framework, which Smith considers unworkable, is now embedded in a high-profile economist consensus statement on AI policy — watch for it resurfacing in actual legislative proposals.
insights.locator: In my post yesterday about the "We Must Act Now" statement

insights.claim: Technological 'steering' — pushing entrepreneurs to invent technologies that complement rather than replace human labor — is the central policy recommendation of Acemoglu and Johnson's 2023 book Power and Progress.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Any future AI industrial policy modeled on Acemoglu's framework will center on government/expert panels picking winning technologies — a concrete policy risk to watch for.
insights.locator: Technological “steering” is the central policy recommendation

themes.theme: Cherry-picked historical examples undermine the book's thesis

insights.claim: Acemoglu and Johnson cite the Haber-Bosch nitrogen fertilizer process as an example of a technology that brought 'nothing like shared prosperity' because it was later adapted into WWI chemical weapons, despite the same chemistry sustaining an estimated 3.5 billion people (about half of Earth's population) via fertilizer.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Illustrates a pattern of selectively chosen historical examples in a book being used to justify AI industrial policy — a flag for weak empirical grounding in the underlying framework.
insights.quote: new inventions that brought nothing like shared prosperity
insights.locator: At the end of the nineteenth century, German chemist Fritz Haber

insights.claim: Contrary to Power and Progress's claim that automating textile technology immiserated 1700s British workers, the same textile automation technologies coincided with rapidly rising wages in China (peak apparel exporter, late 2000s) and Bangladesh, where incomes tripled since 1990.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Undercuts the book's core causal claim that automating technologies inherently immiserate workers — the same tech correlates with rising wages in other contexts, weakening any policy built on that causal story.
insights.locator: China went through a period where it made most of the world’s clothes

insights.claim: The Panama Canal is cited by Acemoglu and Johnson as a 'colossal failure' due to brutal labor exploitation during construction, but Panama today enjoys a standard of living much higher than its Central American neighbors, suggesting the canal itself did generate broad prosperity even if its construction process was unjust.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Shows the book conflates construction-process brutality with technology-outcome failure — a reasoning error investors should watch for when similar 'tech harmed workers' narratives get applied to AI.
insights.locator: A third questionable example in the book is the story of the Panama Canal

insights.claim: Acemoglu and Johnson praise Japanese automakers for combining automation with new task creation and preserving jobs, but Japan's manufacturing wages have been falling since the early 1990s while American manufacturing wages only stagnated, contradicting the book's framing.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: A cited 'success case' for labor-complementing automation doesn't hold up against actual wage data — a reason to discount similar success-story claims in AI-steering advocacy.
insights.locator: A fourth dodgy example is the story they tell about Japan

insights.claim: Book reviewer Brian Potter (cited in comments) notes Power and Progress incorrectly credits Eli Whitney with developing interchangeable parts, a claim that has been widely debunked, reflecting weak scholarship throughout the book.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Basic factual errors in a widely-cited policy-influential book should lower confidence in its broader empirical claims about technology and labor.
insights.quote: it claims at several points that Eli Whitney was responsible for the development of interchangeable parts, a claim that has been widely and thoroughly debunked
insights.locator: Update: In the comments, Brian Potter adds

insights.claim: Power and Progress asserts 'evidence is mounting' that AI could impoverish billions in the developing world, but Smith could not find a supporting citation, and the one relevant paper he knows (Acemoglu, Autor, Hazell and Restrepo 2022) instead found no discernible relationship between AI exposure and employment or wage growth.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: A headline claim underpinning AI-doom policy advocacy lacks empirical backing — investors should be skeptical of similar unsourced 'evidence is mounting' framing in AI policy debates.
insights.quote: We find no discernible relationship between AI exposure and employment or wage growth at the occupation or industry level
insights.locator: For example, in Chapter 1 the authors ask

insights.claim: Acemoglu and Restrepo's own 2020 paper found industrial robots reduced employment and wages, but the same paper's Table A9 found broader IT capital exposure had no effect or a positive effect on employment and wages — a nuance the book omits when claiming 'digital technologies became the graveyard of shared prosperity.'
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: The book selectively reports its own authors' data — a reason to distrust sweeping 'digital tech destroyed prosperity' narratives used to justify AI intervention.
insights.quote: digital technologies became the graveyard of shared prosperity
insights.locator: For example, in Chapter 8, Acemoglu and Johnson argue

insights.claim: A large and growing body of follow-up research (Mann & Püttmann 2018, Dixon/Hong/Wu 2021 on Canada, Koch/Manuylov/Smolka 2019 on Spain, Adachi/Kawaguchi/Saito 2020 on Japan, Eggleston/Lee/Iizuka 2021 on Japanese nursing homes, Hirvonen/Stenhammar/Tuhkuri 2022 on Finland) finds robot adoption correlates with higher, not lower, employment, contradicting Acemoglu and Restrepo's 2020 finding.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: The weight of firm-level evidence globally favors a 'robots complement labor' view over Acemoglu's automation-harms-workers thesis — relevant prior for how AI adoption may play out at firms.
insights.locator: I’ll list a few of these studies

insights.claim: Hötte, Somers, and Theodorakopoulos's 2022 review of the full automation-technology literature finds automation does replace jobs but that this is outweighed by a 'reinstatement' effect where displaced workers find new jobs, and incomes generally rise.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Broad literature review supports a base case of net job/income gains from automation waves, a useful prior for assessing AI's likely aggregate labor effect.
insights.locator: Złooming out from just the manufacturing sector

themes.theme: Conceptual critique: power, productivity, and the 'menu of technologies'

insights.claim: Acemoglu and Johnson define 'power' so broadly (as whatever determines who gets a contested resource) that it collapses persuasion and violent compulsion into one category, making the concept tautological and empirically useless for isolating causality.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: A core analytical concept underlying 'steering' policy is not rigorous — investors evaluating AI-steering proposals should probe whether the underlying theory of 'who has power' is operationalizable at all.
insights.quote: Power is about the ability of an individual or group to achieve explicit or implicit objectives. If two people want the same loaf of bread, power determines who will get it.
insights.locator: Here, in Chapter 3, Acemoglu and Johnson deploy a definition

insights.claim: Both historical eras Power and Progress blames on 'bad' automation (18th century British Industrial Revolution, US since 1970s) also had sluggish productivity growth, suggesting stagnant wages were driven by weak productivity growth rather than automation redirecting gains away from labor.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Reframes the wage-stagnation story as a productivity problem, not a power/distribution problem — implies pro-growth productivity policy, not redistribution-via-steering, is the more direct lever.
insights.locator: But Acemoglu and Johnson also note that both of these eras had sluggish productivity growth

insights.claim: Crafts (2020) finds UK labor productivity grew only about 0.4%/year in the 60 years after 1770 and TFP growth accelerated only gradually to 0.6-0.8%/year by 1830-1860, indicating the Industrial Revolution wage stagnation reflects slow productivity growth, not a shift of gains from labor to capital.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Undermines the direct historical analogy often drawn between the Industrial Revolution and a feared AI-driven wage-productivity divergence.
insights.locator: Regarding the early Industrial Revolution, some researchers argue

insights.claim: Despite rising US inequality since the 1970s, pay has largely kept pace with productivity when benefits are included, and the modest decline in labor's share of national income was mostly driven by rising land values rather than automation.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Challenges the popular 'decoupling of pay from productivity' narrative used to justify interventionist AI/automation policy.
insights.locator: As for the U.S. since the 1970s, inequality has definitely increased

insights.claim: Acemoglu and Johnson coin the term 'so-so automation' for technologies that remove humans from tasks without meaningfully raising productivity, implicitly conceding automation itself may be behind stagnating productivity, yet they never make this the book's central thesis.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: The 'so-so automation' framework is a more defensible and actionable idea than the book's power/steering thesis — worth tracking as a lens for evaluating which AI deployments are genuinely productivity-additive versus merely labor-substituting without gains.
insights.quote: productivity gains from automation may always be somewhat limited
insights.locator: In fact, Acemoglu and Johnson also blame automation for stagnating productivity

insights.claim: Power and Progress never provides a single historical example of a union or government successfully pushing a company to choose a labor-complementing technology over a labor-replacing one from its hypothesized 'menu of technologies', undermining the plausibility that such a menu, or the ability to steer choices on it, actually exists.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: If no historical precedent exists for successfully steering technology choice, current AI-steering policy proposals modeled on this book lack a proof of concept.
insights.locator: As far as I can tell, this book does not contain even one single example

themes.theme: Why 'steering' AI is unworkable as policy

insights.claim: Determining in advance whether a not-yet-invented technology will raise or lower the labor share is effectively impossible, so any 'mandarins in a room' (economists, government engineers) tasked with steering technology would be operating on guesswork.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Direct warning against proposed AI-steering panels or industrial policy bodies — treat such proposals as unlikely to produce useful ex-ante technology selection.
insights.quote: Fundamentally, it still boils down to some sort of mandarins in a room somewhere — economists? government engineers? bloggers? — trying to assess the economic effects of a technology that doesn’t even exist yet.
insights.locator: But how do we know in advance, before a technology is invented

insights.claim: Some of the world's top AI experts predicted AI would replace radiologists within a few years, yet demand for radiologists boomed even as AI tools came online, showing technologists themselves cannot reliably predict a technology's labor-market effects.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: A concrete, falsified prediction case investors can cite whenever new AI-will-replace-X-profession forecasts circulate — treat such forecasts skeptically until realized in hiring/wage data.
insights.locator: Some of the world’s top experts thought that AI would replace radiologists

insights.claim: Smith proposes wage subsidies and labor-market institutions like co-determination and sectoral bargaining as superior ex-post alternatives to ex-ante technology steering, since they raise the labor share without requiring experts to predict unpredictable technology effects, and would act like a Pigouvian incentive rewarding labor-complementing choices automatically.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Identifies a concrete, more implementable policy alternative to AI-steering panels that investors should watch for in place of interventionist tech-selection proposals.
insights.locator: In particular, these solutions seem inferior to something far simpler

insights.claim: A panel of experts empowered to approve or block new technologies would introduce costly delays into corporate decision-making, while Chinese rivals facing no such review would suffer no such delays.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Frames AI-steering regulation as a competitiveness risk versus China specifically — a geopolitical angle investors should weigh against any US AI industrial-policy proposal modeled on Acemoglu's ideas.
insights.locator: And it would be an incredibly costly tax on our companies

themes.theme: The AI wage/employment data so far contradicts the book's fears

insights.claim: US wage inequality has flatlined since the early 2010s, real wages have risen strongly (aside from post-pandemic inflation), production/nonsupervisory worker wages have outpaced managerial wages, and prime-age employment is near all-time highs — all during the exact period AI commercialization exploded (ImageNet 2012, transformer paper 2017, widespread generative AI 2022-23).
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: The empirical backdrop for AI-doom labor policy is currently the opposite of what the 'steering' thesis predicts — a strong reason to be skeptical of aggressive AI labor-market intervention right now.
insights.locator: Meanwhile, real wages have been rising strongly for years now

insights.claim: If people in 2012 had been asked to predict the labor-market impact of a new technology that could recognize objects, imitate speech, and create art, they likely would have expected accelerating inequality — and so far that prediction has been completely wrong.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: A thought experiment cautioning against confident forward-looking claims about AI's labor effects — useful heuristic for discounting current AI-doom forecasts too.
insights.quote: This was not on the menu.
insights.locator: If you went back to 2012, and asked people to predict the impact
